nCerning the campa■gn prov■s■ons
FACTS
You have a campaign comrnittee which you have used for
officeholder expenses. This conmittee made a loan to another
elected officer prior to January 1, 1989. You wish to terminate
the committee in November, 1990.
OUESTION
May the committee forgive the loan or must the conmittee
collect repayment in order to terminate?
CONCLUSION
The committee may not forgive the loan. The loan must be
repaid or you may transfer the outstanding loan frorn the
officeholder comrnittee to another committee controlled by you.
ANALYSIS
A forgiveness of a loan is a contribution. 
(Sectj-on 82015.)
As of January-1, 1989, candidates, inctuding their controlled
committees, are prohibited frorn rnakj.ng contributions out of
carnpaign funds to other candidates. (Section 85304.) Therefore, a
candidate who made a loan from carnpaign funds to another candidate
prior to January 1, 1989, may not forgive the loan. 
(wechsler
Advice Letter, No. I-89-351-, copy enc].osed.)
ll 
Government code sections 81ooo-91015. AIl statutory
references are to the Government code unless otherwise indicated.
Cornmission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations
Section 1sooo, et ses. AI1 references to regul,ations are to Title
2, Division 6 of the california code of Regulatlons.

5(、60


Commission Regulation 18404 (b) provides that a comrnittee may
terninate only if the committee:
(1) Has ceased to receive contributions and
make expenditures and does not anticipate recej.ving
contrj,butions or making expenditures in the future;
(2) Has eliminated or has declared that it
has no intention or ability to discharge all of
its debts, Ioans received and other obligations;
(3) Has no surplus funds; and
(4) Has filed all required campaign state-
ments disclosing aI1 reportable transactions,
You indicated that you wish to terminate the committee
holding the outstanding loan in November, 1990. In order to do so
it will be necessary to collect repa)rnent of the loan or transfer
the loan to another committee controlled by you.
In addition to prohibiting candidates from rnaking
contributions to other candidates, Section 85304 prohibits
candidates from rnaking transfers of funds betr^/een their own
controlled committees. However, in a preliminary injunction
issued by the United States District Court, Eastern District of
California, the court enjoined the Cornmission from enforcing
Section 85304's prohibition against a candidate's transfer of
funds between his or her own committees. (
Practices Commission, No. CM 89-0433 LKK-JFM, May 15, 1989.)
Therefore, a candidate may transfer campaign funds, including a
loan made by a candidate's controlled comrnittee, arnong his or her
own controlied conrnitt ees .2 |
To report transfer of the outstanding l-oan, the terminating
committee's campaign statement (Form 490) must include on Schedule
EE, Part 2 and Part 3 a notation. that a loan transfer has occurred
betr.reen the two cornmittees. e s'imi.lar notation should be included
as a footnote _to Line 9 of the committee's Summary Page.
't 
Section 85201 provides that aII Ioans made to a candidate or
controlled committee must be deposited in the bank account
established for the specific election and that all campa j"gn
expenditures (including loan repayments) nust be made frorn the
sane account. Therefore, a candidate or controlled committee may
not transfer an outstanding loan received by one controlled
cornmittee to another of his/her controlled committees.


Sirnilarly, the canpaign statement of the cornmittee receiving
the outstanding loan would include the sarne information on its
Sumnary Page and on Schedule EE, Part 1. In addition, the
committee receiving the transferred loan will al-so report it on
ScheduLe EE, Part 3 lrhen filing its serni-annual statement covering
the period ending December 31, 1990.
Please do not hesitate to contact me at (916) 322-5662 if you
have additional questions.
sincerely,
Scott HaIlabrin
